source.title: Crane Load Block Failure - Structural Damage Incident

context.region: Gulf of America, South Timbalier Area

event.top_event: Load Block Failure and Dropped Object
actions_taken: Injured person evacuation via motor vessel to Port of Fourchon
Load block and sheave recovery operations
Third-party crane mechanic mobilization on May 10, 2025
Load block, sheave, and retainer shipped for Root Cause Analysis
Crane contractor revision of inspection checklists and procedures
event.summary: On May 9, 2025, at approximately 0900 hours, a crane load block failure occurred during offloading operations at Platform A in Block 229, South Timbalier Area, approximately 50 miles offshore in the Gulf of America. While lowering a Job Safety Analysis document in a trash bag attached to the load block hook, the crane cable lost tension and fell approximately 65 feet onto the aft deck of a motor vessel. The load block sheave was ejected upward and fell onto the vessel deck, striking a grocery box. A deckhand sustained a lumbar muscle strain while evading the falling components. Root cause analysis revealed inadequate lubrication of the main block sheave bearings due to internal debris obstruction in the lubrication passage, leading to bearing failure, sheared dowel pin, and sheave pin dislodgement. Contributing factors included operator inattention to distress indicators, inadequate training on early mechanical failure signs, and unsafe work practices involving maintaining the suspended load at excessive height above the vessel.
recommendations: BSEE Houma District recommends the Office of Incident Investigations issue a Safety Alert
Implement revised Block and Hook Inspection Forms requiring explicit verification of lubrication points serviced
Enhance Crane Pre-Use Inspections to include visual checks of all load block pins, retainers, and hardware
Require flushing and verification of all lubrication pathways during annual, quarterly, and monthly servicing
Implement photographic documentation during annual inspections
Strengthen operator training on early mechanical distress indicators including block rocking, grinding/popping, and reduced wire-rope tension
Establish protocol to remove crane from service immediately upon detection of abnormalities
Apply safe work practice of lowering loads to lowest and safest height above water before swinging over vessel
Conduct hazard analysis to identify and mitigate consequences of suspended loads at height

threats.name: Sheave Bearing Failure due to Inadequate Lubrication
Dowel Pin Shearing from Excessive Rotational Force
Loss of Cable Tension and Load Block Destabilization
Operator Failure to Recognize Distress Indicators
Inadequate Hazard Analysis for Load Positioning

consequences.name: Dropped Load Block and Sheave onto Vessel Deck
Equipment Damage and Loss of Crane Availability
Potential Fatality from Dropped Object Impact

controls.name: Crane Pre-Use Inspection
controls.barrier_role: Detection of mechanical defects prior to operation
controls.lod_basis: Initial barrier to identify equipment degradation before use

supporting_text: Prior to the work beginning, the Job Safety Analysis (JSA) and Crane Pre-Use Inspections were completed.
BSEE Investigators included competency-based questions including how Crane Pre-Use Inspections are completed.
The crane contractor revised its Primary Inspection Checklist and Block and Hook Inspection Form to explicitly require verification of 'lubrication points serviced'

The presence of fresh grease found during both the BSEE IF Investigation and the teardown of the load block at the crane contractor maintenance facility confirmed that standard greasing procedures were performed properly prior to the incident.

However, it was concluded that because the internal lubrication passages were found obstructed beyond the visible and serviceable points, the restriction could not have been identified without full disassembly.

controls.name: Load Block Lubrication and Maintenance Program
controls.barrier_role: Prevent bearing degradation through proper lubrication
controls.lod_basis: Preventive maintenance to maintain mechanical integrity

supporting_text: The W & T/crane contractor inspection and RCA revealed that this incident was 'set into motion by inadequate lubrication of the main block sheave bearings'.

Debris buildup discovered in the internal lubrication passage between the 90-degree transition and the passage outlet, prevented grease lubricant from reaching the pin and bearing, leading to failure of one of the three sheave bearings.
It was confirmed that lubrication is required during annual, quarterly, and monthly inspections, as noted in Section III of the the crane contractor's Primary Inspection Checklist.

The presence of fresh grease found during both the BSEE IF Investigation and the teardown of the load block at the crane contractor maintenance facility confirmed that standard greasing procedures were performed properly prior to the incident.

controls.name: Operator Awareness of Mechanical Distress Indicators
controls.barrier_role: Early detection and intervention based on audible, visual, or tactile warning signs
controls.lod_basis: Human-based detection of equipment degradation during operation

supporting_text: Operators did not recognize or act upon potential audible, visual, or tactile distress indicators (e.g., block rocking, grinding/popping, reduced wire-rope tension) that should have triggered an immediate out-of-service action and inspection.
The investigation identified the need to strengthen operator awareness of early mechanical distress signs and responses (later addressed via safety alerts and training).
The Crane Operator admitted he had never considered this practice when simply lowering a JSA to the MV for review.

W & T and the crane contractor both published internal safety alerts that echoed the same message: moving forward, all crane operators should remain alert to visual, audible, or tactile warning signs such as strange noises, load block tilting or rocking, unusual wire rope slack or tension, or jerky behavior.

controls.name: Anti-Two Block Safety Device
controls.barrier_role: Prevent overload and limit load descent to safe height
controls.lod_basis: Mechanical safeguard to prevent catastrophic overload

supporting_text: As the load block was lifted high enough to clear the bulwarks of the MV, the anti-two block triggered and disabled the 'line-up' function of the winch.
The Crane Mechanic mentioned that to utilize the winch to recover the load block and stinger back onto the top deck of the facility, he had to cap off the anti-two block controls.
hydraulic plumbing associated with the anti-two block was found bent and pulled away from their fixtures.

controls.name: Safe Work Practice - Load Positioning Height
controls.barrier_role: Minimize consequence severity by lowering load to safest height before swinging over vessel

supporting_text: Both the Crane Operator and Rigger answered questions that focused around one seemingly common safe work practice of crane operations in offshore waters: when lowering loads to a MV, lower loads to their lowest and safest height above the water and just high enough to clear any obstructions before swinging the load from above the water to its desired destination on the back deck of the MV.

They acknowledged that the practice minimizes potential severity before proceeding to swing a load over a MV and agreed that the Load Block should never have existed 65 feet above the deck of the boat.
However, the Crane Operator admitted he had never considered this practice when simply lowering a JSA to the MV for review.

Maintaining the load block at approximately 65 ft over the MV during positioning increased potential consequences once failure occurred. Even though it did not cause the mechanical failure, the injury could have been prevented by an adequate hazard analysis that identified the hazard of the suspended load at height and mitigation of lowering the load to its safest and lowest point before swinging over the boat.

controls.name: Hazard Analysis and Job Safety Analysis (JSA)
controls.barrier_role: Identify hazards and establish safe work procedures prior to operations
controls.lod_basis: Planning and risk assessment before work execution

supporting_text: Prior to the work beginning, the Job Safety Analysis (JSA) and Crane Pre-Use Inspections were completed.
The JSA was placed in a kitchen trash bag and attached to the hook of the stinger from the load block in order for it to be lowered down to the MV for review and signature.

Management Systems: Inadequate documentation/procedural specificity- Prior to post-incident revisions, inspection/maintenance documentation did not explicitly require verification of internal lubrication flow paths or detailed retainer condition checks, which reduced the chance of discovering internal degradation short of disassembly.
Management Systems – Inadequate hazard analysis- Unsafe Work Practice increasing consequence severity

controls.name: Operator Training on Equipment and Procedures
controls.barrier_role: Ensure personnel competency in crane operations and hazard recognition
controls.lod_basis: Personnel competency and knowledge of safe practices

supporting_text: Personnel Training: Personnel not trained on equipment used or system operated (awareness depth)- The investigation identified the need to strengthen operator awareness of early mechanical distress signs and responses (later addressed via safety alerts and training).
This gap contributed somewhat to the event by reducing the likelihood of early detection and intervention
The crane contractor issued an internal, company-wide safety alert outlining the incident, new inspection requirements, and early detection indicators for bearing or pin failure.
The crane contractor emphasized the importance of thorough Crane Pre-Use Inspections by crane operators, including visual checks of all load block pins, retainers, and hardware.

controls.name: Crane Lockout and Out-of-Service Procedures
controls.barrier_role: Prevent further operation of failed equipment and enable safe recovery
controls.lod_basis: Immediate containment and isolation of hazard

supporting_text: The crane was immediately secured.
BSEE noted that the crane (stenciled as ZZZ-9000) was flagged Out of Service (OOS), and the controls were locked out.
After the incident, the Rigger locked out the controls on the crane and placed an OOS placard on the crane.
On May 10, 2025, a third-party contracted Crane Mechanic and a W & T Compliance Tech flew to ST-229 to 'safe out' the crane and begin the W & T Investigation.

controls.name: Personnel Evacuation and Emergency Response
controls.barrier_role: Minimize injury severity through rapid medical intervention
controls.lod_basis: Post-incident injury management and medical care

supporting_text: The IP was evacuated via GOL's MV Force to the Port of Fourchon and transported to OMS in Houma, Louisiana, where he received first aid treatment for a lumbar muscle strain and released to full duty.
Following the incident, the cable and load block were lifted high enough to clear them off of the back deck of the MV, releasing the boat from danger and enabling the evacuation of the IP to proceed.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
